edges.description: Immune activation yields circulating antibodies against self tissue antigens.
Neural tissue-reactive antibodies are consistent with immune-mediated motor pathway dysfunction.
Gastrointestinal tissue-reactive antibodies align with mucosal inflammatory injury.
A systemic tissue-reactive autoimmune profile supports immune targeting of hair follicles.
Perturbed neuromuscular signaling produces recurrent painful spasms.
Persistent mucosal inflammation disrupts nutrient and carbohydrate uptake.
Objective absorptive impairment manifests clinically as malabsorption.
Impaired intestinal absorption contributes to persistent diarrhea.
Chronic malabsorptive disease can impair growth.
Follicular immune injury culminates in diffuse hair loss.

nodes.description: Carbohydrate malabsorption is demonstrated by abnormal D-xylose and oral glucose tolerance testing.

evidence.reference_title: Gastrointestinal manifestations in Satoyoshi syndrome: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Chronic diarrhea was the main digestive symptom (92.3%). Other symptoms such as abdominal pain (15.4%), nausea (7.7%) and vomiting (7.7%), were less frequent. The D-xylose test was positive in 10 out of 12 patients, and 9 out of 13 cases showed a flattened oral glucose tolerance test suggesting carbohydrate malabsorption.

evidence.reference_title: Gastrointestinal manifestations in Satoyoshi syndrome: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Chronic diarrhea may be severe and result in malnutrition, anemia, growth retardation, cachexia, disability and even death.

evidence.reference_title: Is Satoyoshi syndrome an autoimmune disease? A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Autoantibody determinations were performed in 39 patients, of which 27 had positive results. The most frequently detected autoantibodies were ANAs.

evidence.reference_title: Satoyoshi syndrome-A case report from India.
evidence.snippet: It is a rare multisystem disorder of presumed autoimmune etiology that is characterized by alopecia, intermittent painful muscle spasms, diarrhea, and antinuclear antibody positivity.

nodes.description: Western blot studies identified circulating antibodies that react with brain, stomach, and duodenal tissue lysates, consistent with a shared tissue-targeted autoimmune response.

evidence.reference_title: Satoyoshi syndrome has antibody against brain and gastrointestinal tissue.
evidence.snippet: A common antibody against brain, stomach, and duodenal tissue, according to Western blot analysis, was detected in the sera of two patients with this syndrome. These findings suggest that Satoyoshi syndrome is a systemic autoimmune disease involving the nervous, endocrine, and gastrointestinal systems.
